category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Unit 4 - Probability and Statistics/Probability Theory

questiontext.text: Bayes' theorem states that for events \(A\) and \(B\) with \(P(B) > 0\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(P(A|B) = \dfrac{P(B|A)P(A)}{P(B)}\)

Bayes' theorem relates the conditional probability \(P(A|B)\) to \(P(B|A)\): \(P(A|B) = \frac{P(B|A)P(A)}{P(B)}\). Here, \(P(A)\) is the prior probability, \(P(B|A)\) is the likelihood, and \(P(A|B)\) is the posterior probability. By the law of total probability: \(P(B) = P(B|A)P(A) + P(B|A^c)P(A^c)\). Bayes' theorem is the foundation of Bayesian inference.

questiontext.text: Two events \(A\) and \(B\) are independent if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)\)

Events \(A\) and \(B\) are (stochastically) independent iff \(P(A \cap B) = P(A)P(B)\). Equivalently (when \(P(B) > 0\)), \(P(A|B) = P(A)\) — knowing \(B\) occurred gives no information about \(A\). Note: independence is different from mutual exclusivity. Mutually exclusive events (with positive probability) are actually negatively correlated (dependent).

questiontext.text: Chebyshev's inequality states that for a random variable \(X\) with mean \(\mu\) and variance \(\sigma^2\), and any \(k > 0\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(P(|X - \mu| \geq k\sigma) \leq \dfrac{1}{k^2}\)

Chebyshev's inequality: \(P(|X-\mu| \geq k\sigma) \leq 1/k^2\). This holds for any distribution with finite variance — no knowledge of the distribution shape is required. For \(k=2\): at least 75% of values lie within 2 standard deviations; for \(k=3\): at least 89%. Chebyshev's inequality is used to prove the Weak Law of Large Numbers.

questiontext.text: The Central Limit Theorem states that if \(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n\) are i.i.d. with mean \(\mu\) and variance \(\sigma^2\), then as \(n \to \infty\), the distribution of \(\dfrac{\bar{X}_n - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}}\) converges to:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) Standard normal distribution \(N(0,1)\)

The Central Limit Theorem (CLT): \(\frac{\bar{X}_n - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}} \xrightarrow{d} N(0,1)\) as \(n \to \infty\), regardless of the distribution of \(X_i\) (provided \(\mu\) and \(\sigma^2\) are finite). This explains why the normal distribution appears so frequently in statistics. The CLT is the theoretical justification for using normal-based inference for large samples.

questiontext.text: The Weak Law of Large Numbers states that for i.i.d. random variables \(X_1, X_2, \ldots\) with mean \(\mu\), as \(n \to \infty\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(\bar{X}_n \xrightarrow{P} \mu\) (converges in probability)

WLLN: \(\bar{X}_n = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n X_i \xrightarrow{P} \mu\), i.e., for any \(\epsilon > 0\), \(P(|\bar{X}_n - \mu| > \epsilon) \to 0\). The Strong LLN gives almost sure convergence: \(\bar{X}_n \xrightarrow{a.s.} \mu\). The WLLN requires finite variance (Chebyshev's proof), but can be proved with only finite mean (Kolmogorov's proof). This justifies using the sample mean as an estimate of the population mean.

category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Unit 4 - Probability and Statistics/Probability Distributions

questiontext.text: If \(X \sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda)\), then the mean and variance of \(X\) are respectively:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) Both equal to \(\lambda\)

For \(X \sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda)\): \(P(X=k) = e^{-\lambda}\lambda^k/k!\) for \(k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots\). The mean \(E[X] = \lambda\) and variance \(\text{Var}(X) = \lambda\). This equal mean-variance property is a defining characteristic of the Poisson distribution. It arises as a limit of the Binomial distribution when \(n \to \infty\), \(p \to 0\), and \(np \to \lambda\).

questiontext.text: If \(X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)\), then \(P(\mu - 2\sigma < X < \mu + 2\sigma)\) is approximately:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) 0.9545 (approximately 95%)

The empirical rule (68-95-99.7 rule) for normal distributions:
• \(P(\mu - \sigma < X < \mu + \sigma) \approx 0.6827\) (68.27%)
• \(P(\mu - 2\sigma < X < \mu + 2\sigma) \approx 0.9545\) (95.45%)
• \(P(\mu - 3\sigma < X < \mu + 3\sigma) \approx 0.9973\) (99.73%)
This follows from \(P(-2 < Z < 2) = 2\Phi(2) - 1 \approx 2(0.9772) - 1 = 0.9544\).

questiontext.text: The exponential distribution \(\text{Exp}(\lambda)\) has the memoryless property, which means:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(P(X > s + t \mid X > s) = P(X > t)\)

The memoryless property: \(P(X > s+t \mid X > s) = P(X > t)\) for all \(s, t > 0\). This means "given that the event hasn't occurred in the first \(s\) time units, the probability that it takes at least \(t\) more time units is the same as if we started fresh." The exponential distribution is the only continuous distribution with this property; the geometric distribution is its discrete analogue. It is used to model inter-arrival times in Poisson processes.

answer.text: \(P(X > s + t \mid X > s) = P(X > t)\)
\(P(X > s + t) = P(X > s) \cdot P(X > t)\)
The distribution does not depend on \(\lambda\)

questiontext.text: If \(X \sim B(n, p)\), the variance of \(X\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(np(1-p)\)

For \(X \sim B(n,p)\): \(P(X=k) = \binom{n}{k}p^k(1-p)^{n-k}\). The mean is \(E[X] = np\) and variance is \(\text{Var}(X) = np(1-p) = npq\) where \(q = 1-p\). The variance is maximized at \(p = 1/2\), giving \(\text{Var} = n/4\). The Binomial can be approximated by \(N(np, np(1-p))\) for large \(n\) (CLT) or by \(\text{Poisson}(np)\) when \(p\) is small.

questiontext.text: If \(Z_1, Z_2, \ldots, Z_n\) are i.i.d. \(N(0,1)\), then \(\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^n Z_i^2\) follows a:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) Chi-squared distribution with \(n\) degrees of freedom

\(\sum_{i=1}^n Z_i^2 \sim \chi^2(n)\). The chi-squared distribution with \(n\) df has mean \(n\) and variance \(2n\). It is used in: testing goodness of fit (\(\chi^2\) test), testing independence, confidence intervals for variance (\(s^2(n-1)/\sigma^2 \sim \chi^2(n-1)\)). The sum of independent chi-squared variables is chi-squared with degrees of freedom equal to the sum.

answer.text: Chi-squared distribution with \(n\) degrees of freedom

category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Unit 4 - Probability and Statistics/Statistical Inference

questiontext.text: The Maximum Likelihood Estimator (MLE) of the parameter \(p\) in a Binomial distribution \(B(n, p)\), based on observing \(x\) successes in \(n\) trials, is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(\hat{p} = x/n\)

The likelihood is \(L(p) = \binom{n}{x} p^x (1-p)^{n-x}\). Taking the log and differentiating: \(\frac{d\ln L}{dp} = \frac{x}{p} - \frac{n-x}{1-p} = 0\), giving \(\hat{p} = x/n\) (the sample proportion). The MLE is unbiased (\(E[\hat{p}] = p\)) and efficient (achieves the Cramér-Rao lower bound). MLEs are invariant under reparametrization.

questiontext.text: An estimator \(\hat{\theta}\) of a parameter \(\theta\) is said to be unbiased if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(E[\hat{\theta}] = \theta\)

An estimator is unbiased if its expected value equals the true parameter. The bias is \(B(\hat{\theta}) = E[\hat{\theta}] - \theta\). For example, the sample mean \(\bar{X}\) is an unbiased estimator of the population mean \(\mu\), and \(S^2 = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum(X_i-\bar{X})^2\) is unbiased for \(\sigma^2\) (note the \(n-1\) denominator). The MSE = Variance + Bias²; for unbiased estimators, MSE = Variance.

questiontext.text: The Cramér-Rao lower bound states that for any unbiased estimator \(\hat{\theta}\) of \(\theta\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq \dfrac{1}{I(\theta)}\)

The Cramér-Rao inequality: \(\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq \frac{1}{nI(\theta)}\) where \(I(\theta) = E\!\left[\left(\frac{\partial \ln f(X;\theta)}{\partial \theta}\right)^2\right]\) is the Fisher information. An estimator achieving this bound is called efficient (or UMVUE). MLEs are asymptotically efficient. The bound cannot be achieved for all distributions; when it can, the distribution belongs to the exponential family.

questiontext.text: A 95% confidence interval for the population mean \(\mu\), based on a sample of size \(n\) with sample mean \(\bar{x}\) and known population standard deviation \(\sigma\), is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(\left(\bar{x} - 1.96\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}},\; \bar{x} + 1.96\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\right)\)

The 95% CI for \(\mu\) when \(\sigma\) is known: \(\bar{x} \pm z_{0.025} \cdot \sigma/\sqrt{n} = \bar{x} \pm 1.96 \sigma/\sqrt{n}\), since \(z_{0.025} = 1.96\) (the 97.5th percentile of \(N(0,1)\)). Interpretation: if we repeat the sampling many times, 95% of such intervals will contain the true \(\mu\). When \(\sigma\) is unknown, replace \(\sigma\) by \(s\) and \(1.96\) by \(t_{n-1,0.025}\).

category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Unit 4 - Probability and Statistics/Testing of Hypotheses

name.text: Hypothesis Testing - Type I and Type II Errors

questiontext.text: In hypothesis testing, a Type I error occurs when:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) We reject \(H_0\) when \(H_0\) is actually true

Types of errors in hypothesis testing:
• Type I error (False Positive): Reject \(H_0\) when \(H_0\) is true. Probability = \(\alpha\) (significance level).
• Type II error (False Negative): Fail to reject \(H_0\) when \(H_0\) is false. Probability = \(\beta\).
• Power = \(1 - \beta\) = probability of correctly rejecting a false \(H_0\).
There is a trade-off: decreasing \(\alpha\) (Type I error) increases \(\beta\) (Type II error) for fixed sample size.

answer.text: We reject \(H_0\) when \(H_0\) is actually true
We fail to reject \(H_0\) when \(H_0\) is false

questiontext.text: The Neyman-Pearson lemma states that the most powerful test of size \(\alpha\) for testing a simple null \(H_0: \theta = \theta_0\) against a simple alternative \(H_1: \theta = \theta_1\) is the:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) Likelihood ratio test that rejects \(H_0\) when \(L(\theta_1)/L(\theta_0) > k\)

Neyman-Pearson Lemma: the most powerful test of size \(\alpha\) rejects \(H_0\) when the likelihood ratio \(\Lambda = L(\theta_1)/L(\theta_0) > k_\alpha\), where \(k_\alpha\) is chosen so that \(P(\Lambda > k_\alpha \mid H_0) = \alpha\). This is the uniformly most powerful (UMP) test for simple vs. simple hypotheses. For one-sided composite alternatives in exponential families, UMP tests exist via the monotone likelihood ratio property.

question.single: truetrueabc Test that maximizes the Type II error Likelihood ratio test that rejects \(H_0\) when \(L(\theta_1)/L(\theta_0) > k\) Test based on the sample mean only Test that minimizes the sample size

name.text: Hypothesis Testing - Chi-square Goodness of Fit

questiontext.text: In a chi-square goodness-of-fit test with \(k\) categories and no estimated parameters, the degrees of freedom are:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(k - 1\)

The chi-square statistic \(\chi^2 = \sum_{i=1}^k \frac{(O_i - E_i)^2}{E_i}\) has approximately a \(\chi^2\) distribution with \(k-1\) degrees of freedom when no parameters are estimated (one constraint: \(\sum O_i = n\)). If \(m\) parameters are estimated from the data, the df becomes \(k - 1 - m\). This test compares observed frequencies \(O_i\) with expected frequencies \(E_i\) under \(H_0\).

questiontext.text: The \(p\)-value of a hypothesis test is defined as:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) The probability, under \(H_0\), of obtaining a test statistic at least as extreme as the observed one

The \(p\)-value = \(P(\text{observed statistic or more extreme} \mid H_0)\). We reject \(H_0\) at significance level \(\alpha\) iff \(p \leq \alpha\). A small \(p\)-value provides evidence against \(H_0\). Importantly, the \(p\)-value is NOT the probability that \(H_0\) is true — it is the probability of the data (or more extreme data) given \(H_0\).

answer.text: The probability, under \(H_0\), of a test statistic at least as extreme as the observed value

category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Unit 4 - Probability and Statistics/Markov Chains and Stochastic Processes

questiontext.text: A stochastic process \(\{X_n\}_{n \geq 0}\) is a Markov chain if it satisfies the Markov property:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(P(X_{n+1} = j \mid X_n = i, X_{n-1}, \ldots, X_0) = P(X_{n+1} = j \mid X_n = i)\)

The Markov property ("memoryless" property for stochastic processes): the future state depends only on the present state, not on how we arrived there. This is the key property that makes Markov chains tractable. The transition probability \(p_{ij} = P(X_{n+1}=j \mid X_n=i)\) defines the transition matrix \(P = (p_{ij})\), where \(P^n\) gives n-step transition probabilities.

answer.text: \(P(X_{n+1} = j \mid X_n = i, X_{n-1}, \ldots, X_0) = P(X_{n+1} = j \mid X_n = i)\)
\(P(X_{n+1} = j) = P(X_n = i)\) for all \(i, j\)

questiontext.text: A probability distribution \(\pi\) is a stationary distribution of a Markov chain with transition matrix \(P\) if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(\pi P = \pi\) (i.e., \(\sum_i \pi_i p_{ij} = \pi_j\) for all \(j\))

A stationary (invariant) distribution \(\pi\) satisfies \(\pi P = \pi\) (vector-matrix form) or equivalently \(\pi_j = \sum_i \pi_i p_{ij}\) for all states \(j\), with \(\sum_j \pi_j = 1\) and \(\pi_j \geq 0\). For irreducible, positive recurrent Markov chains, a unique stationary distribution exists and equals the long-run proportions of time spent in each state (ergodic theorem). For detailed balance (reversibility): \(\pi_i p_{ij} = \pi_j p_{ji}\).

answer.text: \(\pi P = \pi\) (i.e., \(\pi\) is a left eigenvector of \(P\) with eigenvalue 1)

questiontext.text: A state \(i\) of a Markov chain is said to be recurrent if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) The probability of returning to state \(i\) starting from \(i\) is 1

State \(i\) is recurrent if \(f_{ii} = P(\text{ever return to } i \mid X_0 = i) = 1\). It is transient if \(f_{ii} < 1\). Equivalently, \(i\) is recurrent iff \(\sum_{n=0}^\infty p_{ii}^{(n)} = \infty\) (the expected number of returns is infinite). A recurrent state is positive recurrent if the mean return time \(\mu_i = E[\text{return time to } i \mid X_0=i] < \infty\), and null recurrent if \(\mu_i = \infty\).

answer.text: The probability of returning to state \(i\) is 0
The chain stays in state \(i\) forever
The probability of returning to state \(i\) starting from \(i\) is 1
The mean return time to \(i\) is finite

questiontext.text: A Poisson process \(\{N(t)\}_{t \geq 0}\) with rate \(\lambda > 0\) has the property that the number of arrivals in a time interval of length \(t\) follows a:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) Poisson distribution with mean \(\lambda t\)

Key properties of a Poisson process with rate \(\lambda\): (1) \(N(0)=0\); (2) Independent increments; (3) Stationary increments: \(N(t+s)-N(s) \sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda t)\); (4) Inter-arrival times \(T_i \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda)\) and are i.i.d. The Poisson process is the canonical model for random arrivals (radioactive decay, telephone calls, etc.) and is the basis for more general birth-death processes.

category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Unit 4 - Probability and Statistics/Sampling Theory

questiontext.text: In simple random sampling (SRS) without replacement from a population of size \(N\) with mean \(\mu\) and variance \(\sigma^2\), the variance of the sample mean \(\bar{x}\) based on a sample of size \(n\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(\dfrac{\sigma^2}{n} \cdot \dfrac{N-n}{N-1}\)

For SRS without replacement: \(\text{Var}(\bar{x}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{n} \cdot \frac{N-n}{N-1}\). The factor \(\frac{N-n}{N-1}\) is the finite population correction (fpc). When \(n/N \to 0\) (small sampling fraction), fpc \(\approx 1\) and the formula reduces to the i.i.d. case \(\sigma^2/n\). The fpc reflects the reduction in variance due to sampling without replacement (we "know" more about the population as \(n \to N\)).

questiontext.text: In stratified random sampling with proportional allocation, the sample size from stratum \(h\) (of size \(N_h\)) is chosen as:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(n_h = n \cdot N_h / N\)

Proportional allocation: each stratum contributes to the sample in proportion to its size in the population, so \(n_h = n \cdot N_h/N\). This ensures the sample mirrors the population structure (self-weighting sample). Optimal (Neyman) allocation additionally considers within-stratum variability: \(n_h \propto N_h \sigma_h\), minimizing total variance for a given total sample size. Stratified sampling generally achieves lower variance than SRS when strata are homogeneous internally.

answer.text: \(n_h = n / H\) (equal allocation, \(H\) = number of strata)

questiontext.text: In systematic sampling from a population of \(N = 1000\) units with a sample of size \(n = 50\), we select every \(k\)-th unit where \(k\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) 20

The sampling interval is \(k = N/n = 1000/50 = 20\). In systematic sampling, we randomly select a starting point \(r\) from 1 to \(k\), then select units \(r, r+k, r+2k, \ldots\). This is simpler to implement than SRS and often more efficient when the population is randomly ordered. However, if there is periodicity in the population matching the sampling interval, the sample may be biased.

questiontext.text: Spearman's rank correlation coefficient \(r_s\) is calculated using the formula (where \(d_i\) is the difference in ranks and \(n\) is the number of pairs):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(r_s = 1 - \dfrac{6\sum d_i^2}{n(n^2-1)}\)

Spearman's rank correlation: \(r_s = 1 - \frac{6\sum d_i^2}{n(n^2-1)}\), where \(d_i = R(X_i) - R(Y_i)\) is the difference between the ranks of \(X_i\) and \(Y_i\). It ranges from \(-1\) (perfect negative ranking) to \(+1\) (perfect positive ranking). It is a non-parametric measure of monotone association, useful when data are ordinal or when the Pearson correlation's normality assumption is not met.

Answer: (C) 0.4

\(P(A|B)=P(A\cap B)/P(B)=0.2/0.5=0.4\). Note that here \(P(A|B)=P(A)\), so \(A\) and \(B\) are independent events.

questiontext.text: Bayes' theorem states that \(P(A_i|B)=\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(\frac{P(B|A_i)P(A_i)}{\sum_j P(B|A_j)P(A_j)}\)

Bayes' formula combines the likelihood \(P(B|A_i)\) with the prior \(P(A_i)\) to give the posterior \(P(A_i|B)\). The denominator is the total probability \(P(B)\) by the law of total probability.

questiontext.text: Events \(A_1,A_2,\ldots,A_n\) are mutually independent if and only if for every subset \(\{i_1,\ldots,i_k\}\),

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(P(A_{i_1}\cap\cdots\cap A_{i_k})=P(A_{i_1})\cdots P(A_{i_k})\)

Mutual independence requires the multiplication rule to hold for all subsets (not just pairs). Pairwise independence does not imply mutual independence — a classic counterexample involves three events where each pair is independent but all three together are not.

answer.text: \(P(A_i\cap A_j)=P(A_i)P(A_j)\) for all \(i\neq j\) (pairwise independence only)

questiontext.text: A probability space is a triple \((\Omega,\mathcal{F},P)\) where \(\mathcal{F}\) is a \(\sigma\)-algebra, meaning it is closed under

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) complementation and countable unions

A \(\sigma\)-algebra \(\mathcal{F}\) satisfies: (i) \(\Omega\in\mathcal{F}\); (ii) if \(A\in\mathcal{F}\) then \(A^c\in\mathcal{F}\); (iii) if \(A_1,A_2,\ldots\in\mathcal{F}\) then \(\bigcup_{n=1}^\infty A_n\in\mathcal{F}\). These imply closure under countable intersections as well.

answer.text: All set operations including uncountable unions

questiontext.text: The first Borel-Cantelli lemma states that if \(\sum_{n=1}^\infty P(A_n)<\infty\), then

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(P(A_n\text{ i.o.})=0\) (almost surely only finitely many \(A_n\) occur)

"i.o." means "infinitely often" = \(\limsup_n A_n=\bigcap_{n=1}^\infty\bigcup_{k=n}^\infty A_k\). By the first BC lemma, summability of probabilities implies at most finitely many events occur a.s. The second lemma (requiring independence) gives the converse under divergence.

questiontext.text: The characteristic function of a random variable \(X\) is defined as \(\phi_X(t)=E[e^{itX}]\). It uniquely determines

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) the distribution of \(X\)

The characteristic function \(\phi_X(t)=\int e^{itx}\,dF(x)\) is the Fourier transform of the distribution. By the uniqueness theorem for Fourier transforms, \(\phi_X\) uniquely determines the distribution \(F\). It always exists (unlike the moment generating function).

answer.text: Only the mean and variance of \(X\)
The density function only if \(X\) is continuous

name.text: Probability Theory - Law of Total Expectation

questiontext.text: The law of total expectation (tower property) states that \(E[X]=\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(E[E[X|Y]]\)

The tower property: \(E[X]=E[E[X|Y]]\) for any random variable \(Y\). More generally, \(E[E[X|\mathcal{G}]]=E[X]\) for any sub-\(\sigma\)-algebra \(\mathcal{G}\). This is fundamental in conditional expectation theory.

questiontext.text: Jensen's inequality states that for a convex function \(\phi\) and a random variable \(X\),

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(\phi(E[X])\leq E[\phi(X)]\)

Jensen's inequality: if \(\phi\) is convex, \(\phi(E[X])\leq E[\phi(X)]\). For concave \(\phi\), the inequality reverses. Applications: \(e^{E[X]}\leq E[e^X]\) (convexity of \(e^x\)), \(E[\ln X]\leq\ln E[X]\) (concavity of \(\ln\)).

questiontext.text: The correct hierarchy of convergence modes (stronger implies weaker) for sequences of random variables is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) a.s. \(\Rightarrow\) in probability \(\Rightarrow\) in distribution

Implications: (i) a.s. convergence \(\Rightarrow\) convergence in probability; (ii) \(L^p\) convergence \(\Rightarrow\) convergence in probability; (iii) convergence in probability \(\Rightarrow\) convergence in distribution. None of the converses hold in general.

name.text: Probability Theory - Strong Law of Large Numbers

questiontext.text: The Strong Law of Large Numbers (SLLN) states that for i.i.d. random variables \(X_1,X_2,\ldots\) with \(E[|X_1|]<\infty\),

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(\bar X_n=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{k=1}^n X_k\to\mu\) almost surely

The SLLN (Kolmogorov): finite first moment is necessary and sufficient for a.s. convergence of the sample mean to \(\mu=E[X_1]\). This is stronger than the Weak LLN (convergence in probability), which requires only finite variance under Chebyshev's inequality.

questiontext.text: The Central Limit Theorem (Lindeberg-Lévy) states: if \(X_1,\ldots,X_n\) are i.i.d. with mean \(\mu\) and variance \(\sigma^2<\infty\), then as \(n\to\infty\),

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(\frac{\sqrt{n}(\bar X_n-\mu)}{\sigma}\xrightarrow{d}N(0,1)\)

The CLT: the standardized sample mean converges in distribution to the standard normal. This holds regardless of the underlying distribution (as long as finite variance exists). The convergence rate is \(O(n^{-1/2})\) (Berry-Esseen theorem).

questiontext.text: If the MGF \(M_X(t)=E[e^{tX}]\) exists in a neighborhood of \(t=0\), then the \(k\)-th moment is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(M_X^{(k)}(0)\)

Differentiating \(M_X(t)=\sum_{k=0}^\infty\frac{E[X^k]}{k!}t^k\) gives \(M_X^{(k)}(0)=E[X^k]\). The MGF uniquely determines the distribution (when it exists in a neighborhood of 0) and is related to the characteristic function by \(M_X(t)=\phi_X(-it)\).

questiontext.text: Chebyshev's inequality states that for any random variable \(X\) with mean \(\mu\) and variance \(\sigma^2\),

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(P(|X-\mu|\geq k\sigma)\leq 1/k^2\)

Chebyshev's inequality: \(P(|X-\mu|\geq\varepsilon)\leq\sigma^2/\varepsilon^2\). Setting \(\varepsilon=k\sigma\) gives \(P(|X-\mu|\geq k\sigma)\leq 1/k^2\). This is distribution-free and is used to prove the Weak Law of Large Numbers.

questiontext.text: If \(X\sim\text{Binomial}(n,p)\), then \(E[X]\) and \(\text{Var}(X)\) are respectively

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(np\) and \(np(1-p)\)

For \(X=\sum_{i=1}^n X_i\) with i.i.d. Bernoulli\((p)\) summands: \(E[X]=np\), \(\text{Var}(X)=np(1-p)=npq\). The variance is maximized at \(p=1/2\).

questiontext.text: If \(X\sim\text{Poisson}(\lambda)\), then \(P(X=k)=\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(\frac{e^{-\lambda}\lambda^k}{k!}\)

The Poisson distribution models the number of rare events in a fixed interval. Its PMF is \(P(X=k)=e^{-\lambda}\lambda^k/k!\) for \(k=0,1,2,\ldots\) Both mean and variance equal \(\lambda\).

questiontext.text: If \(X\sim N(\mu,\sigma^2)\), the density is \(f(x)=\frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-(x-\mu)^2/(2\sigma^2)}\). The MGF of \(X\) is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(e^{\mu t+\sigma^2 t^2/2}\)

For \(X\sim N(\mu,\sigma^2)\): \(M_X(t)=E[e^{tX}]=\exp(\mu t+\sigma^2t^2/2)\). This is derived by completing the square in the exponent. The log of the MGF, \(\mu t+\sigma^2t^2/2\), is the cumulant generating function (CGF).

questiontext.text: The exponential distribution \(\text{Exp}(\lambda)\) is the unique continuous distribution with the memoryless property: \(P(X>s+t|X>s)=\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(P(X>t)\)

Memoryless property: \(P(X>s+t|X>s)=P(X>t)\). The exponential is the only continuous distribution with this property (the geometric distribution is the discrete analogue). Mean \(=1/\lambda\), variance \(=1/\lambda^2\).

questiontext.text: If \(Z_1,\ldots,Z_n\) are i.i.d. \(N(0,1)\), then \(\sum_{i=1}^n Z_i^2\) follows

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(\chi^2(n)\) (chi-squared with \(n\) degrees of freedom)

The chi-squared distribution with \(n\) degrees of freedom is \(\chi^2(n)=\text{Gamma}(n/2,1/2)\). Mean \(=n\), variance \(=2n\). It is fundamental in statistics (sample variance, goodness-of-fit tests, independence tests).

questiontext.text: If \(Z\sim N(0,1)\) and \(V\sim\chi^2(n)\) are independent, then \(T=Z/\sqrt{V/n}\) follows

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) Student's \(t\)-distribution with \(n\) degrees of freedom

The \(t(n)\) distribution arises from \(T=Z/\sqrt{V/n}\). It is symmetric, bell-shaped, with heavier tails than normal. As \(n\to\infty\), \(t(n)\to N(0,1)\). Used for inference about means with unknown variance.

questiontext.text: If \(U\sim\chi^2(m)\) and \(V\sim\chi^2(n)\) are independent, then \(F=\frac{U/m}{V/n}\) follows

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(F(m,n)\) distribution

The \(F(m,n)\) distribution is the ratio of two independent chi-squared distributions divided by their respective degrees of freedom. It is used in ANOVA and to compare variances. Note: \(F(1,n)=t(n)^2\) and \(F(m,n)^{-1}\sim F(n,m)\).

questiontext.text: The Gamma distribution \(\text{Gamma}(\alpha,\beta)\) has density \(f(x)=\frac{\beta^\alpha}{\Gamma(\alpha)}x^{\alpha-1}e^{-\beta x}\) for \(x>0\). Its mean and variance are

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(\alpha/\beta\) and \(\alpha/\beta^2\)

For \(\text{Gamma}(\alpha,\beta)\) (rate parameterization): \(E[X]=\alpha/\beta\), \(\text{Var}(X)=\alpha/\beta^2\). Special cases: \(\text{Exp}(\lambda)=\text{Gamma}(1,\lambda)\), \(\chi^2(n)=\text{Gamma}(n/2,1/2)\).

questiontext.text: The Beta distribution \(\text{Beta}(\alpha,\beta)\) on \([0,1]\) has density \(f(x)\propto x^{\alpha-1}(1-x)^{\beta-1}\). Its mean is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(\alpha/(\alpha+\beta)\)

For \(\text{Beta}(\alpha,\beta)\): \(E[X]=\frac{\alpha}{\alpha+\beta}\), \(\text{Var}(X)=\frac{\alpha\beta}{(\alpha+\beta)^2(\alpha+\beta+1)}\). The Beta distribution is the conjugate prior for the Binomial likelihood in Bayesian statistics.

questiontext.text: For a multivariate normal vector \(\mathbf{X}\sim N(\boldsymbol\mu,\Sigma)\), any linear combination \(\mathbf{a}^T\mathbf{X}\) is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(N(\mathbf{a}^T\boldsymbol\mu,\mathbf{a}^T\Sigma\mathbf{a})\)

The multivariate normal is closed under linear transformations: if \(\mathbf{X}\sim N(\boldsymbol\mu,\Sigma)\), then \(A\mathbf{X}+\mathbf{b}\sim N(A\boldsymbol\mu+\mathbf{b},A\Sigma A^T)\). For a scalar combination: \(\mathbf{a}^T\mathbf{X}\sim N(\mathbf{a}^T\boldsymbol\mu,\mathbf{a}^T\Sigma\mathbf{a})\).

questiontext.text: If \(X\) counts the number of Bernoulli trials until the first success (with success probability \(p\)), then \(E[X]=\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(1/p\)

For the geometric distribution \(P(X=k)=(1-p)^{k-1}p\) (\(k=1,2,\ldots\)): \(E[X]=1/p\), \(\text{Var}(X)=(1-p)/p^2\). The geometric distribution is the discrete analogue of the exponential (memoryless: \(P(X>m+n|X>m)=P(X>n)\)).

questiontext.text: The negative binomial distribution \(\text{NB}(r,p)\) models the number of trials until the \(r\)-th success. Its mean is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(r/p\)

For NB\((r,p)\): \(E[X]=r/p\), \(\text{Var}(X)=r(1-p)/p^2\). It is a generalization of the geometric (case \(r=1\)). Also arises as a Poisson-Gamma mixture (overdispersed Poisson), making it useful in count data modeling.

questiontext.text: If \(X\sim\text{Uniform}(a,b)\), then \(\text{Var}(X)=\)
The Cauchy distribution \(C(0,1)\) with density \(f(x)=\frac{1}{\pi(1+x^2)}\) is notable because

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (D) it has no finite mean or variance

The Cauchy distribution has heavy tails: \(\int_{-\infty}^\infty |x|/(1+x^2)\,dx=\infty\), so \(E[|X|]=\infty\). The sample mean of Cauchy i.i.d. variables is again Cauchy (CLT fails). The Cauchy is a stable distribution with index 1.

answer.text: It has mean 0 but infinite variance
It has no finite mean or variance

questiontext.text: A population of \(N\) items contains \(K\) successes. If \(n\) items are drawn without replacement, the number of successes \(X\) follows a hypergeometric distribution with mean

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(nK/N\)

For the hypergeometric distribution: \(E[X]=nK/N\) (same as binomial with \(p=K/N\)), \(\text{Var}(X)=n\frac{K}{N}\frac{N-K}{N}\frac{N-n}{N-1}\). The factor \((N-n)/(N-1)\) is the finite population correction.

questiontext.text: An estimator \(\hat\theta\) of \(\theta\) is called unbiased if

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(E[\hat\theta]=\theta\) for all \(\theta\)

An estimator is unbiased if its expected value equals the true parameter for all values of \(\theta\). The bias is \(B(\hat\theta)=E[\hat\theta]-\theta\). The sample mean \(\bar X\) is unbiased for \(\mu\); the sample variance \(S^2=\sum(X_i-\bar X)^2/(n-1)\) is unbiased for \(\sigma^2\).

questiontext.text: The Cramér-Rao lower bound states that for any unbiased estimator \(\hat\theta\),

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(\text{Var}(\hat\theta)\geq 1/I(\theta)\)

The CRLB: \(\text{Var}(\hat\theta)\geq[I(\theta)]^{-1}\) where \(I(\theta)=-E[\partial^2\ln f/\partial\theta^2]=E[(\partial\ln f/\partial\theta)^2]\) is the Fisher information. An estimator achieving the CRLB is called efficient (e.g., MLE for exponential families in large samples).

questiontext.text: For an i.i.d. sample from \(N(\mu,\sigma^2)\) (both unknown), the MLE of \(\sigma^2\) is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(\hat\sigma^2=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n(X_i-\bar X)^2\)

Maximizing the log-likelihood gives \(\hat\mu=\bar X\) and \(\hat\sigma^2=\frac{1}{n}\sum(X_i-\bar X)^2\). Note: the MLE of \(\sigma^2\) is biased (divides by \(n\), not \(n-1\)). The unbiased estimator \(S^2\) divides by \(n-1\).

questiontext.text: A statistic \(T(X)\) is sufficient for \(\theta\) if the conditional distribution of \(X\) given \(T(X)\) is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) independent of \(\theta\)

Sufficiency: \(T(X)\) is sufficient for \(\theta\) iff \(f(x|\theta)=g(T(x)|\theta)\cdot h(x)\) (Fisher-Neyman factorization theorem). Intuitively, \(T\) contains all information about \(\theta\) in the sample — knowing \(T\) renders the data uninformative about \(\theta\).

questiontext.text: The Rao-Blackwell theorem states that if \(\hat\theta\) is an unbiased estimator and \(T\) is a sufficient statistic, then \(\tilde\theta=E[\hat\theta|T]\) is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) unbiased with \(\text{Var}(\tilde\theta)\leq\text{Var}(\hat\theta)\)

Rao-Blackwell: conditioning on a sufficient statistic cannot increase variance. The Lehmann-Scheffé theorem further shows that conditioning on a complete sufficient statistic gives the UMVUE (uniformly minimum variance unbiased estimator).

questiontext.text: A 95% confidence interval for \(\mu\) based on a sample from \(N(\mu,\sigma^2)\) with known \(\sigma^2\) is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(\bar X\pm 1.96\,\sigma/\sqrt{n}\)

Since \(\bar X\sim N(\mu,\sigma^2/n)\), the standardized statistic \(Z=(\bar X-\mu)/(\sigma/\sqrt{n})\sim N(0,1)\). The 95% CI uses \(z_{0.025}=1.96\): \(P(\bar X-1.96\sigma/\sqrt{n}\leq\mu\leq\bar X+1.96\sigma/\sqrt{n})=0.95\).

questiontext.text: A family of distributions \(\{f(x;\theta)\}\) is an exponential family if \(f(x;\theta)=h(x)\exp[\eta(\theta)T(x)-B(\theta)]\). The natural sufficient statistic is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(T(x)\)

By the Neyman-Fisher factorization, \(T(x)\) is a sufficient statistic for \(\theta\) in any exponential family. Moreover, for a full-rank exponential family, \(T(x)\) is complete sufficient, so the UMVUE of any estimable function can be derived from \(T\).

questiontext.text: The Neyman-Pearson lemma states that for testing \(H_0:\theta=\theta_0\) vs \(H_1:\theta=\theta_1\), the most powerful test of size \(\alpha\) rejects \(H_0\) when

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) the likelihood ratio \(\Lambda=f(x;\theta_1)/f(x;\theta_0)>k\)

The NP lemma guarantees that the likelihood ratio test (LRT) is the most powerful (MP) test of given size. The rejection region \(\Lambda>k\) maximizes power \(P(\text{reject }H_0|\theta_1)\) subject to \(P(\text{reject }H_0|\theta_0)=\alpha\).

answer.text: The sample mean exceeds a threshold
The sample variance exceeds a threshold

name.text: Testing of Hypotheses - Type I and Type II Errors

questiontext.text: In hypothesis testing, a Type I error is rejecting \(H_0\) when it is true (probability \(\alpha\)), and a Type II error is failing to reject \(H_0\) when it is false (probability \(\beta\)). The power of a test is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(1-\beta\)

Power \(=1-\beta=P(\text{reject }H_0|H_1\text{ true})\). There is a fundamental trade-off: for fixed sample size, decreasing \(\alpha\) increases \(\beta\) (decreases power). Increasing sample size reduces both errors simultaneously.

questiontext.text: The \(p\)-value of a test statistic \(T_{\text{obs}}\) is defined as

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(P(T\geq T_{\text{obs}}|H_0)\) (for a right-tailed test)

The \(p\)-value is the probability, under \(H_0\), of observing a test statistic at least as extreme as the one observed. We reject \(H_0\) at level \(\alpha\) iff the \(p\)-value \(\leq\alpha\). A small \(p\)-value provides evidence against \(H_0\).

answer.text: \(P(T\geq T_{\text{obs}}\mid H_0)\) for a right-tailed test

questiontext.text: A uniformly most powerful (UMP) test exists for one-sided testing in exponential families with monotone likelihood ratio (MLR). For \(H_0:\theta\leq\theta_0\) vs \(H_1:\theta>\theta_0\), the UMP test rejects when

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) the sufficient statistic \(T\) exceeds a critical value \(c\)

For exponential families \(f(x;\theta)\propto\exp[\eta(\theta)T(x)]\) with \(\eta\) increasing in \(\theta\) (MLR in \(T\)), the UMP test for \(H_0:\theta\leq\theta_0\) vs \(H_1:\theta>\theta_0\) rejects when \(T>c\). This is Karlin-Rubin's theorem.

answer.text: The sufficient statistic \(T\) exceeds a critical value \(c\)
The likelihood ratio is less than \(k\)
The sample mean is less than \(\theta_0\)

name.text: Testing of Hypotheses - Chi-squared Goodness of Fit

questiontext.text: Pearson's chi-squared goodness-of-fit statistic \(\chi^2=\sum_{i=1}^k\frac{(O_i-E_i)^2}{E_i}\) has, under \(H_0\), approximately a chi-squared distribution with

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(k-1-r\) degrees of freedom, where \(r\) is the number of estimated parameters

For Pearson's chi-squared test with \(k\) cells: if no parameters are estimated, df \(=k-1\). If \(r\) parameters are estimated from the data (by MLE from grouped data), df \(=k-1-r\). The loss of 1 df comes from the constraint \(\sum O_i=n\).

answer.text: \(k-1-r\), where \(r\) is the number of estimated parameters

questiontext.text: For testing \(H_0:\mu_1=\mu_2\) with two independent samples from \(N(\mu_i,\sigma^2)\) (equal, unknown variance), the pooled two-sample \(t\)-statistic is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(t=\frac{\bar X_1-\bar X_2}{S_p\sqrt{1/n_1+1/n_2}}\sim t(n_1+n_2-2)\)

The pooled variance estimate is \(S_p^2=[(n_1-1)S_1^2+(n_2-1)S_2^2]/(n_1+n_2-2)\). Under \(H_0\), the test statistic follows a \(t\)-distribution with \(n_1+n_2-2\) degrees of freedom.

name.text: Testing of Hypotheses - Likelihood Ratio Test

questiontext.text: Wilks' theorem states that under regularity conditions, the statistic \(-2\ln\Lambda\) (where \(\Lambda\) is the likelihood ratio) converges in distribution under \(H_0\) to

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(\chi^2(r)\), where \(r\) is the number of constraints under \(H_0\)

Wilks' theorem: \(-2\ln\Lambda=-2[\ell(\hat\theta_0)-\ell(\hat\theta)]\xrightarrow{d}\chi^2(r)\) under \(H_0\), where \(r=\dim\Theta-\dim\Theta_0\) is the number of restrictions. This provides the asymptotic basis for the generalized LRT.

answer.text: \(\chi^2(r)\), where \(r\) is the number of constraints under \(H_0\)

questiontext.text: In one-way ANOVA with \(k\) groups and total \(n\) observations, the \(F\)-statistic for testing equality of group means is the ratio of

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) mean sum of squares between groups (MSB) to mean sum of squares within groups (MSW)

ANOVA \(F\)-statistic: \(F=\text{MSB}/\text{MSW}\sim F(k-1,n-k)\) under \(H_0:\mu_1=\cdots=\mu_k\). MSB \(=\text{SSB}/(k-1)\), MSW \(=\text{SSW}/(n-k)\). Large \(F\) provides evidence against equal means.

answer.text: Mean SS between groups (MSB) to mean SS within groups (MSW)

questiontext.text: The sign test for the median \(M\) of a continuous distribution uses the test statistic \(B=\) number of observations greater than \(M_0\). Under \(H_0:M=M_0\),

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(B\sim\text{Binomial}(n,1/2)\)

Since each observation independently exceeds \(M_0\) with probability 1/2 under \(H_0\) (continuous distribution), \(B\sim\text{Binomial}(n,1/2)\). The sign test is distribution-free (nonparametric) and valid for any continuous distribution.

questiontext.text: The Kolmogorov-Smirnov test statistic \(D_n=\sup_x|F_n(x)-F_0(x)|\) (where \(F_n\) is the empirical CDF) is used to test

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(H_0:F=F_0\) (the distribution is \(F_0\))

The KS test is a nonparametric goodness-of-fit test. Under \(H_0\), \(\sqrt{n}D_n\) converges to the Kolmogorov distribution. The test is distribution-free (for continuous \(F_0\)) and consistent against any fixed alternative.

answer.text: That two samples have equal means

questiontext.text: The Bonferroni correction for \(m\) simultaneous tests at family-wise error rate \(\alpha\) rejects individual \(H_{0i}\) when the individual \(p\)-value satisfies

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(p_i\leq\alpha/m\)

Bonferroni correction: by the union bound, \(P(\text{any false rejection})\leq\sum_{i=1}^m P(\text{reject }H_{0i}|H_{0i}\text{ true})\leq m\cdot(\alpha/m)=\alpha\). This controls the family-wise error rate (FWER) but is conservative (low power).

questiontext.text: The Wilcoxon rank-sum test (Mann-Whitney \(U\)-test) is a nonparametric alternative to the two-sample \(t\)-test. It is based on

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) the ranks of all observations in the combined sample

The Wilcoxon test uses ranks of the combined sample. Under \(H_0\) (equal distributions), the ranks are equally likely to be any permutation. The test is distribution-free and has high efficiency relative to the \(t\)-test for heavy-tailed distributions.

answer.text: The absolute deviations from the median
The ranks of all observations in the combined sample
The signs of differences from the hypothesized median
The squared deviations from the sample mean

questiontext.text: In Bayesian hypothesis testing, the Bayes factor \(BF_{01}\) for comparing \(H_0\) vs \(H_1\) is defined as

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) the ratio of marginal likelihoods \(p(x|H_0)/p(x|H_1)\)

The Bayes factor \(BF_{01}=p(x|H_0)/p(x|H_1)\) measures the evidence in the data for \(H_0\) relative to \(H_1\). The posterior odds equal the Bayes factor times the prior odds: \(P(H_0|x)/P(H_1|x)=BF_{01}\cdot P(H_0)/P(H_1)\).

questiontext.text: A discrete-time Markov chain \(\{X_n\}\) satisfies the Markov property, which states

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(P(X_{n+1}=j|X_0,\ldots,X_n)=P(X_{n+1}=j|X_n)\)

The Markov property: given the present state \(X_n\), the future \(X_{n+1},X_{n+2},\ldots\) is independent of the past \(X_0,\ldots,X_{n-1}\). This is the "memoryless" property for discrete-time processes.

questiontext.text: A probability distribution \(\pi\) is stationary for a Markov chain with transition matrix \(P\) if

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(\pi P=\pi\) (equivalently \(\pi_j=\sum_i\pi_i P_{ij}\))

A stationary distribution satisfies \(\pi=\pi P\): if the chain starts in distribution \(\pi\), it remains in \(\pi\) at all future times. For irreducible, positive recurrent chains, a unique stationary distribution exists. The mean return time to state \(j\) is \(1/\pi_j\).

questiontext.text: For an irreducible, aperiodic, positive recurrent Markov chain, the ergodic theorem states that regardless of the initial state,

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(P_{ij}^{(n)}\to\pi_j\) as \(n\to\infty\)

The ergodic (limit) theorem: for ergodic chains (irreducible + aperiodic + positive recurrent), the \(n\)-step transition probabilities converge to the unique stationary distribution: \(P^n_{ij}\to\pi_j\) for all \(i,j\). Geometrically fast for finite chains.

answer.text: The chain returns to its initial state infinitely often

questiontext.text: State \(i\) of a Markov chain is recurrent if and only if

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(\sum_{n=0}^\infty P_{ii}^{(n)}=\infty\)

State \(i\) is recurrent iff \(\sum_n P_{ii}^{(n)}=\infty\) (the chain returns to \(i\) infinitely often a.s.), or equivalently \(f_{ii}=P(\text{return to }i|X_0=i)=1\). If \(\sum_n P_{ii}^{(n)}<\infty\), state \(i\) is transient (returns finitely many times a.s.).

questiontext.text: A Markov chain is reversible (with respect to \(\pi\)) if it satisfies detailed balance:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(\pi_i P_{ij}=\pi_j P_{ji}\) for all \(i,j\)

Detailed balance (reversibility): the flux from \(i\) to \(j\) equals the flux from \(j\) to \(i\) in stationarity. If detailed balance holds, then \(\pi\) is stationary (summing over \(j\) gives \(\sum_j\pi_i P_{ij}=\sum_j\pi_j P_{ji}=\pi_i\)). Used in MCMC (Metropolis-Hastings).

questiontext.text: In a Markov chain with absorbing states, the expected number of steps to absorption from transient state \(i\) is given by the \(i\)-th row sum of

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) the fundamental matrix \(N=(I-Q)^{-1}\)

For a chain with transient states governed by sub-stochastic matrix \(Q\), the fundamental matrix \(N=(I-Q)^{-1}=\sum_{n=0}^\infty Q^n\). Entry \(N_{ij}\) is the expected number of visits to transient state \(j\) starting from \(i\). Row sums give expected absorption times.

questiontext.text: A Poisson process \(N(t)\) with rate \(\lambda\) satisfies: \(N(0)=0\), independent increments, and

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(N(t+s)-N(s)\sim\text{Poisson}(\lambda t)\)

The Poisson process has stationary, independent increments with \(N(t+s)-N(s)\sim\text{Poisson}(\lambda t)\). Interarrival times are i.i.d. Exp\((\lambda)\). It is the unique counting process with stationary independent increments and no simultaneous events.

questiontext.text: Standard Brownian motion (Wiener process) \(W(t)\) is characterized by: \(W(0)=0\), continuous paths, independent increments, and

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(W(t+s)-W(s)\sim N(0,t)\)

Brownian motion has Gaussian increments: \(W(t)-W(s)\sim N(0,t-s)\) for \(t>s\). It has a.s. continuous but nowhere differentiable paths, and is a martingale. Key properties: \(E[W(t)]=0\), \(\text{Var}(W(t))=t\), \(\text{Cov}(W(s),W(t))=\min(s,t)\).

questiontext.text: A sequence \(\{X_n,\mathcal{F}_n\}\) is a martingale if \(E[|X_n|]<\infty\) and

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(E[X_{n+1}|\mathcal{F}_n]=X_n\) a.s.

Martingale condition: the best prediction of \(X_{n+1}\) given past information is \(X_n\) (fair game). Sub-martingale: \(E[X_{n+1}|\mathcal{F}_n]\geq X_n\). Super-martingale: \(\leq X_n\). Examples: \(W(t)\) (Brownian motion), \(W(t)^2-t\), \(S_n=\sum X_i\) with \(E[X_i]=0\).

questiontext.text: Doob's optional stopping theorem states that for a martingale \(\{X_n\}\) and stopping time \(\tau\), under suitable conditions,

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(E[X_\tau]=E[X_0]\)

The optional stopping theorem: if \(\tau\) is a stopping time satisfying integrability conditions (e.g., bounded \(\tau\), or bounded increments, or \(E[\tau]<\infty\) with bounded increments), then \(E[X_\tau]=E[X_0]\). This rules out gambling systems that "beat" fair games.

questiontext.text: A birth-death chain on \(\{0,1,2,\ldots\}\) with birth rates \(\lambda_i\) and death rates \(\mu_i\) has stationary distribution (when it exists)

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(\pi_n=\pi_0\prod_{i=0}^{n-1}\frac{\lambda_i}{\mu_{i+1}}\)

Detailed balance for birth-death chains: \(\pi_n\lambda_n=\pi_{n+1}\mu_{n+1}\), giving \(\pi_{n+1}=(\lambda_n/\mu_{n+1})\pi_n\). Solving recursively: \(\pi_n=\pi_0\prod_{i=0}^{n-1}\lambda_i/\mu_{i+1}\). The stationary distribution exists iff \(\sum_n\pi_n<\infty\).

questiontext.text: The elementary renewal theorem states that for a renewal process with mean inter-renewal time \(\mu=E[X_1]<\infty\),

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(m(t)/t\to 1/\mu\) as \(t\to\infty\)

The elementary renewal theorem: \(m(t)=E[N(t)]\sim t/\mu\) as \(t\to\infty\). The renewal reward theorem extends this: if \(R_i\) is the reward in cycle \(i\), the long-run reward rate is \(E[R]/\mu\). These are the continuous-time analogues of the ergodic theorem.

questiontext.text: Itô's formula for a smooth function \(f(t,W_t)\) where \(W_t\) is Brownian motion gives

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(df=f_t\,dt+f_W\,dW+\frac{1}{2}f_{WW}\,dt\)

Itô's formula (stochastic chain rule): \(df(t,W_t)=\frac{\partial f}{\partial t}dt+\frac{\partial f}{\partial W}dW_t+\frac{1}{2}\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial W^2}dt\). The extra \(\frac{1}{2}f_{WW}dt\) term arises because \((dW)^2=dt\) (quadratic variation of Brownian motion).

questiontext.text: For an M/M/1 queue with arrival rate \(\lambda\) and service rate \(\mu\), the steady-state average number of customers in the system is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(\rho/(1-\rho)\) where \(\rho=\lambda/\mu\)

For the M/M/1 queue, the stationary distribution is geometric: \(\pi_n=(1-\rho)\rho^n\) for \(\rho=\lambda/\mu<1\). The average number in system is \(L=\sum_{n=0}^\infty n\pi_n=\rho/(1-\rho)\). By Little's law: \(L=\lambda W\), so average sojourn time \(W=1/(\mu-\lambda)\).

questiontext.text: In simple random sampling without replacement from a population of size \(N\), the variance of the sample mean \(\bar y\) based on sample size \(n\) is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(\frac{S^2}{n}\cdot\frac{N-n}{N-1}\)

Variance of \(\bar y\) under SRSWOR: \(V(\bar y)=\frac{S^2}{n}\left(1-\frac{n}{N}\right)\) where \(S^2=\frac{\sum(y_i-\bar Y)^2}{N-1}\) is the population variance and \((1-n/N)=(N-n)/N\) is the finite population correction (FPC).

questiontext.text: In stratified random sampling, the optimal (Neyman) allocation of sample size \(n_h\) to stratum \(h\) is proportional to

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(N_h S_h\) (stratum size times stratum standard deviation)

Neyman allocation: \(n_h\propto N_h S_h\). This minimizes \(\text{Var}(\bar y_{st})\) for fixed total \(n=\sum n_h\). Proportional allocation uses \(n_h\propto N_h\). Optimal allocation gives smaller variance than proportional, which in turn is better than equal allocation for heterogeneous strata.

questiontext.text: The ratio estimator \(\hat R=\bar y/\bar x\) for the population ratio \(R=\bar Y/\bar X\) is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) biased but consistent

The ratio estimator \(\hat R=\bar y/\bar x\) is biased (bias \(\approx-\text{Cov}(\bar y,\bar x)/\bar X+R\text{Var}(\bar x)/\bar X^2\)) but the bias is \(O(1/n)\), so \(\hat R\to R\) in probability (consistent). Bias decreases as sample size grows.

questiontext.text: In systematic sampling, the sampling interval is \(k=N/n\). The method selects units \(r, r+k, r+2k,\ldots\) where \(r\) is chosen at random from \(\{1,\ldots,k\}\). A disadvantage is that

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) it may be inefficient if the population has periodic patterns with the same period as \(k\)

If the population has a periodic trend with period \(k\), systematic sampling always hits the same phase of the cycle, giving a highly non-representative sample. For random-order populations, systematic sampling is approximately as efficient as SRSWOR.

answer.text: It requires a complete sampling frame sorted in a specific order
It may be inefficient if the population has periodic patterns matching \(k\)
It cannot be used for large populations
The sample mean is always biased

questiontext.text: Compared to simple random sampling of the same size, cluster sampling is generally

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) less efficient (larger variance) when units within clusters are similar

Cluster sampling efficiency depends on the intra-cluster correlation \(\rho\): when clusters are internally homogeneous (units within a cluster are similar, i.e., \(\rho>0\)), cluster sampling is less efficient than SRS. The design effect is approximately \(1+(m-1)\rho\) where \(m\) is cluster size.

answer.text: Less efficient (larger variance) when units within clusters are similar
More efficient when cluster sizes vary widely

questiontext.text: The regression estimator of the population mean \(\bar Y\) using auxiliary variable \(x\) with known mean \(\bar X\) is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(\hat{\bar Y}_{reg}=\bar y+\hat b(\bar X-\bar x)\)

The regression estimator adjusts \(\bar y\) using the known population mean \(\bar X\) of the auxiliary variable: \(\hat{\bar Y}_{reg}=\bar y+\hat b(\bar X-\bar x)\) where \(\hat b=s_{xy}/s_x^2\). It has smaller variance than \(\bar y\) when \(|\rho_{XY}|>0\), and is approximately unbiased.

questiontext.text: For jointly continuous \((X,Y)\) with joint density \(f(x,y)\), the conditional expectation \(E[X|Y=y]\) is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(\int x\frac{f(x,y)}{f_Y(y)}\,dx\)

The conditional density of \(X\) given \(Y=y\) is \(f_{X|Y}(x|y)=f(x,y)/f_Y(y)\). The conditional expectation is \(E[X|Y=y]=\int x f_{X|Y}(x|y)\,dx=\int xf(x,y)\,dx/f_Y(y)\).

questiontext.text: Markov's inequality states that for a non-negative random variable \(X\) and \(a>0\),

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(P(X\geq a)\leq E[X]/a\)

Markov's inequality: \(P(X\geq a)\leq E[X]/a\) for \(X\geq 0\). It follows from \(E[X]\geq E[X\cdot\mathbf{1}_{X\geq a}]\geq a\cdot P(X\geq a)\). Chebyshev's inequality is derived from Markov's applied to \((X-\mu)^2\).

questiontext.text: In Bayesian estimation, the posterior distribution \(\pi(\theta|x)\propto\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(L(\theta;x)\pi(\theta)\)

Bayes' theorem for inference: posterior \(\propto\) likelihood \(\times\) prior. The posterior \(\pi(\theta|x)\propto L(\theta;x)\pi(\theta)\) summarizes all information about \(\theta\) after observing \(x\). Point estimates: posterior mean (minimizes squared error loss), posterior median (minimizes absolute error), posterior mode (MAP).

questiontext.text: If \(X\sim N(\mu,\sigma^2)\), then \(Y=e^X\) follows a log-normal distribution. The mean of \(Y\) is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(e^{\mu+\sigma^2/2}\)

\(E[Y]=E[e^X]=M_X(1)=e^{\mu+\sigma^2/2}\) (using the MGF of the normal). The log-normal mean exceeds the median \(e^\mu\) due to right skewness. Log-normal models incomes, stock prices, and other multiplicative phenomena.

questiontext.text: A sequence of distribution functions \(F_n\) converges weakly to \(F\) (i.e., \(F_n\xrightarrow{w}F\)) if

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(F_n(x)\to F(x)\) at every continuity point \(x\) of \(F\)

Weak convergence (convergence in distribution): \(F_n\xrightarrow{w}F\) means \(F_n(x)\to F(x)\) for all continuity points of \(F\). Portmanteau theorem gives equivalent conditions: \(E[g(X_n)]\to E[g(X)]\) for all bounded continuous \(g\).

answer.text: \(F_n(x)\to F(x)\) at every continuity point \(x\) of \(F\)

questiontext.text: A distribution is called stable if sums of i.i.d. copies have the same distribution up to location and scale. The only stable distribution with finite variance is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) the normal distribution

Stable distributions are characterized by stability index \(\alpha\in(0,2]\). Only \(\alpha=2\) gives finite variance — the normal distribution. For \(\alpha<2\), the variance is infinite (e.g., Cauchy: \(\alpha=1\), Lévy: \(\alpha=1/2\)). The generalized CLT shows sums of i.i.d. heavy-tailed variables converge to stable laws.

questiontext.text: The delta method states that if \(\sqrt{n}(\hat\theta-\theta)\xrightarrow{d}N(0,\sigma^2)\), then for a smooth function \(g\),

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(\sqrt{n}(g(\hat\theta)-g(\theta))\xrightarrow{d}N(0,[g'(\theta)]^2\sigma^2)\)

The delta method uses a first-order Taylor expansion: \(g(\hat\theta)\approx g(\theta)+g'(\theta)(\hat\theta-\theta)\). This gives the asymptotic distribution of \(g(\hat\theta)\), useful for constructing CIs for nonlinear functions of parameters.

questiontext.text: A Gaussian process \(\{X_t\}_{t\in T}\) is completely characterized by its

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) mean function \(m(t)=E[X_t]\) and covariance function \(K(s,t)=\text{Cov}(X_s,X_t)\)

For a Gaussian process, all finite-dimensional distributions are multivariate normal, fully determined by the mean and covariance functions. Brownian motion: \(m(t)=0\), \(K(s,t)=\min(s,t)\). The Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process: \(K(s,t)=\frac{\sigma^2}{2\theta}e^{-\theta|t-s|}\).

answer.text: The characteristic function at each time point

name.text: Testing of Hypotheses - Fisher Exact Test

questiontext.text: Fisher's exact test for a \(2\times 2\) contingency table computes the \(p\)-value using

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) the hypergeometric distribution

Given fixed marginal totals, under the null of independence, the cell count \(X\) follows a hypergeometric distribution. Fisher's exact test computes the exact \(p\)-value as \(P(\text{table as extreme or more}|H_0,\text{fixed margins})\). No large-sample approximation is needed.

questiontext.text: The Chapman-Kolmogorov equations state that

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(P_{ij}^{(m+n)}=\sum_k P_{ik}^{(m)}P_{kj}^{(n)}\)

Chapman-Kolmogorov: the \((m+n)\)-step transition probabilities satisfy \(P^{(m+n)}=P^{(m)}P^{(n)}\) (matrix product), or componentwise \(P_{ij}^{(m+n)}=\sum_k P_{ik}^{(m)}P_{kj}^{(n)}\). This follows from the law of total probability and the Markov property.

questiontext.text: If \(X\) has characteristic function \(\phi(t)\), the distribution function can be recovered by the inversion formula. For a continuous density \(f\),

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(f(x)=\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\infty}^\infty e^{-itx}\phi(t)\,dt\)

The Fourier inversion formula recovers the density from the characteristic function: \(f(x)=\frac{1}{2\pi}\int e^{-itx}\phi(t)\,dt\) when \(\phi\in L^1\). This is the Fourier inversion theorem applied to probability distributions.

questiontext.text: The method of moments estimator sets the first \(k\) population moments equal to the corresponding sample moments. For the Gamma(\(\alpha,\beta\)) distribution, the MOM estimator of \(\alpha\) is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(\hat\alpha=\bar X^2/S^2\)

Gamma(\(\alpha,\beta\)): \(E[X]=\alpha/\beta\), \(\text{Var}(X)=\alpha/\beta^2\). So \(\alpha=(\mu/\sigma)^2\) and \(\beta=\mu/\sigma^2\). MOM: replace \(\mu\to\bar X\), \(\sigma^2\to S^2\), giving \(\hat\alpha=\bar X^2/S^2\) and \(\hat\beta=\bar X/S^2\).

questiontext.text: Kolmogorov's zero-one law states that for independent events, any tail event \(A\) (an event in \(\bigcap_{n=1}^\infty\sigma(X_{n+1},X_{n+2},\ldots)\)) has probability

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) either 0 or 1

Kolmogorov's 0-1 law: the tail \(\sigma\)-algebra of independent random variables is trivial (every tail event has probability 0 or 1). Examples: \(P(\sum X_n\text{ converges})\in\{0,1\}\), \(P(\limsup X_n/\sqrt{2\log n}\leq 1)=1\) (Law of the Iterated Logarithm).

questiontext.text: The Dirichlet distribution \(\text{Dir}(\alpha_1,\ldots,\alpha_k)\) is the conjugate prior for

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) the Multinomial distribution

Dirichlet-Multinomial conjugacy: if \(\mathbf{p}\sim\text{Dir}(\boldsymbol\alpha)\) and \(\mathbf{X}|\mathbf{p}\sim\text{Multinomial}(n,\mathbf{p})\), then \(\mathbf{p}|\mathbf{X}\sim\text{Dir}(\boldsymbol\alpha+\mathbf{X})\). The Dirichlet generalizes the Beta (case \(k=2\)) and is used in LDA topic models and Bayesian nonparametrics.

questiontext.text: The Ornstein-Uhlenbeck (OU) process \(dX_t=-\theta X_t\,dt+\sigma\,dW_t\) (\(\theta>0\)) has stationary distribution

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(N(0,\sigma^2/(2\theta))\)

The OU process is the unique stationary Gaussian Markov process. Its SDE has solution \(X_t=X_0e^{-\theta t}+\sigma\int_0^t e^{-\theta(t-s)}\,dW_s\). The stationary distribution is \(N(0,\sigma^2/(2\theta))\), approached exponentially fast at rate \(\theta\).

questiontext.text: Akaike's Information Criterion (AIC) selects the model minimizing \(AIC=-2\hat\ell+2k\) where \(\hat\ell\) is the maximized log-likelihood and \(k\) is the number of parameters. The \(2k\) penalty term

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) penalizes model complexity to prevent overfitting

AIC balances goodness-of-fit \(-2\hat\ell\) against model complexity \(2k\). BIC uses penalty \(k\ln n\) (stronger for large \(n\)), selecting more parsimonious models. AIC targets predictive accuracy; BIC targets model identification consistency. Neither is uniformly better.

answer.text: Penalizes model complexity to prevent overfitting
Equals the log-likelihood at the null model

questiontext.text: The cumulants \(\kappa_r\) of a distribution are defined via \(\ln M_X(t)=\sum_{r=1}^\infty\frac{\kappa_r}{r!}t^r\). The third cumulant \(\kappa_3\) measures

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) skewness (asymmetry)

Key cumulants: \(\kappa_1=\mu\) (mean), \(\kappa_2=\sigma^2\) (variance), \(\kappa_3=E[(X-\mu)^3]\) (third central moment, measures skewness), \(\kappa_4=E[(X-\mu)^4]-3\sigma^4\) (excess kurtosis). For the normal distribution, all cumulants of order \(\geq 3\) are zero.

questiontext.text: In probability proportional to size (PPS) sampling, the Horvitz-Thompson estimator of the population total \(Y=\sum_{i=1}^N y_i\) is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(\hat Y_{HT}=\sum_{i\in s}\frac{y_i}{\pi_i}\)

The Horvitz-Thompson estimator: \(\hat Y_{HT}=\sum_{i\in s}y_i/\pi_i\) where \(\pi_i=P(i\in s)\) is the inclusion probability of unit \(i\). It is unbiased for \(Y\) under any design. For SRSWOR: \(\pi_i=n/N\) and \(\hat Y_{HT}=N\bar y\).

name.text: Stochastic Processes - Law of the Iterated Logarithm

questiontext.text: The Law of the Iterated Logarithm (LIL) for i.i.d. \(X_i\) with mean 0 and variance 1 states

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(\limsup_{n\to\infty}\frac{S_n}{\sqrt{2n\log\log n}}=1\) a.s.

The LIL gives the precise fluctuation rate of random walks: \(S_n/\sqrt{2n\log\log n}\) oscillates between \(-1\) and \(+1\) infinitely often. This is sharper than the CLT (\(S_n/\sqrt{n}\to N(0,1)\)) and the SLLN (\(S_n/n\to 0\)).

question.single: truetrueabc \(\limsup_{n\to\infty}\dfrac{S_n}{\sqrt{n}}=1\) a.s. \(\limsup_{n\to\infty}\dfrac{S_n}{\sqrt{n\log n}}=1\) a.s. \(\limsup_{n\to\infty}\dfrac{S_n}{\sqrt{2n\log\log n}}=1\) a.s. \(\limsup_{n\to\infty}\dfrac{S_n}{n}=1\) a.s.

questiontext.text: Under regularity conditions, the MLE \(\hat\theta_n\) is consistent, meaning

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(\hat\theta_n\xrightarrow{P}\theta_0\) as \(n\to\infty\)

The MLE is consistent (converges in probability to the true \(\theta_0\)) and asymptotically normal: \(\sqrt{n}(\hat\theta_n-\theta_0)\xrightarrow{d}N(0,I(\theta_0)^{-1})\). It achieves the Cramér-Rao lower bound asymptotically (asymptotic efficiency).

questiontext.text: Lévy's continuity theorem states that \(X_n\xrightarrow{d}X\) if and only if

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(\phi_{X_n}(t)\to\phi_X(t)\) for all \(t\), where \(\phi\) denotes the characteristic function

Lévy's theorem: convergence in distribution is equivalent to pointwise convergence of characteristic functions. This is the main tool for proving the CLT: compute \(\phi_{S_n/\sqrt{n}}(t)\to e^{-t^2/2}=\phi_{N(0,1)}(t)\), hence \(S_n/\sqrt{n}\to N(0,1)\).

questiontext.text: For i.i.d. sample of size \(n\) from CDF \(F\) with density \(f\), the density of the \(k\)-th order statistic \(X_{(k)}\) is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(\frac{n!}{(k-1)!(n-k)!}[F(x)]^{k-1}[1-F(x)]^{n-k}f(x)\)

The density of \(X_{(k)}\) is derived by considering that exactly \(k-1\) observations fall below \(x\), one at \(x\), and \(n-k\) above \(x\): \(f_{X_{(k)}}(x)=\frac{n!}{(k-1)!(n-k)!}F(x)^{k-1}[1-F(x)]^{n-k}f(x)\).

questiontext.text: \(X\) and \(Y\) are conditionally independent given \(Z\) if

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(f(x,y|z)=f(x|z)f(y|z)\) for all \(x,y,z\)

Conditional independence: \(X\perp Y|Z\) means knowing \(Z\) renders \(X\) and \(Y\) independent. This is the basis of graphical models (Bayesian networks, Markov random fields). Note: conditional independence does not imply marginal independence and vice versa.

questiontext.text: The Sequential Probability Ratio Test (SPRT) of Wald continues sampling while the likelihood ratio \(\Lambda_n\) satisfies \(B<\Lambda_n

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(\Lambda_n\leq B\) (accept \(H_0\)) or \(\Lambda_n\geq A\) (reject \(H_0\))

Wald's SPRT: boundaries \(A=(1-\beta)/\alpha\) and \(B=\beta/(1-\alpha)\). The SPRT minimizes expected sample size among all tests with the same error rates \(\alpha,\beta\). This is the fundamental sequential testing procedure.

answer.text: \(n\geq n_0\) for some fixed sample size \(n_0\)

questiontext.text: The Metropolis-Hastings algorithm generates a Markov chain with stationary distribution \(\pi\) by accepting proposed moves from \(q(y|x)\) with probability

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(\min\!\left(1,\frac{\pi(y)q(x|y)}{\pi(x)q(y|x)}\right)\)

The MH acceptance ratio ensures detailed balance \(\pi(x)q(y|x)\alpha(x,y)=\pi(y)q(x|y)\alpha(y,x)\). This guarantees \(\pi\) is stationary. Special case: symmetric proposal \(q(y|x)=q(x|y)\) gives Metropolis algorithm with \(\alpha=\min(1,\pi(y)/\pi(x))\).

questiontext.text: In simple linear regression \(Y_i=\beta_0+\beta_1 x_i+\varepsilon_i\), the OLS estimator of \(\beta_1\) is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(\hat\beta_1=S_{xy}/S_{xx}\) where \(S_{xy}=\sum(x_i-\bar x)(Y_i-\bar Y)\)

The OLS estimator minimizes \(\sum(Y_i-\hat Y_i)^2\). The solution is \(\hat\beta_1=S_{xy}/S_{xx}\) where \(S_{xx}=\sum(x_i-\bar x)^2\). Under normal errors, this is also the MLE and the BLUE (Gauss-Markov theorem).

questiontext.text: A mixture distribution \(F(x)=\sum_{k=1}^K w_k F_k(x)\) (with \(w_k>0\), \(\sum w_k=1\)) has mean

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(\sum_{k=1}^K w_k\mu_k\) where \(\mu_k=E[X|Z=k]\)

For a mixture with component means \(\mu_k\): \(E[X]=\sum_k w_k\mu_k\) (weighted average of component means). The variance is \(\text{Var}(X)=\sum_k w_k(\sigma_k^2+\mu_k^2)-(\sum_k w_k\mu_k)^2\) (variance + between-component variance).

questiontext.text: A random variable \(\tau\) is a stopping time with respect to a filtration \(\{\mathcal{F}_n\}\) if

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(\{\tau=n\}\in\mathcal{F}_n\) for all \(n\)

A stopping time's occurrence at time \(n\) is determined by the information available up to time \(n\). Equivalently, \(\{\tau\leq n\}\in\mathcal{F}_n\). Examples: first passage times, first time a process exceeds a threshold. Stopping times are fundamental in optional stopping and the strong Markov property.

questiontext.text: The bootstrap estimates the sampling distribution of a statistic \(\hat\theta\) by

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) resampling with replacement from the observed sample and computing \(\hat\theta^*\) on each resample

Efron's bootstrap: draw \(B\) samples of size \(n\) with replacement from \(\{x_1,\ldots,x_n\}\), compute \(\hat\theta^*_b\) for each. The bootstrap distribution of \(\hat\theta^*-\hat\theta\) approximates the sampling distribution of \(\hat\theta-\theta\). Used for CIs, bias/variance estimation, without distributional assumptions.

answer.text: Using the normal approximation to the sampling distribution
Sampling without replacement from the population
Resampling with replacement from the observed data and computing \(\hat\theta^*\) on each resample
Parametric simulation from the fitted model

questiontext.text: The coupling inequality states that for two probability measures \(\mu,\nu\) and any coupling \((X,Y)\) with marginals \(\mu,\nu\),

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(\|\mu- u\|_{TV}\leq P(X eq Y)\)

The coupling inequality: total variation distance \(\|\mu- u\|_{TV}=\sup_A|\mu(A)- u(A)|\leq P(X eq Y)\) for any coupling. The optimal (maximal) coupling achieves equality. Coupling is a key tool in Markov chain mixing time analysis.

questiontext.text: The entropy of a discrete random variable \(X\) with PMF \(p(x)\) is \(H(X)=-\sum_x p(x)\log p(x)\). It is maximized when

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(X\) has a uniform distribution over its support

Maximum entropy: over all distributions on \(k\) outcomes, the uniform distribution \(p(x)=1/k\) maximizes entropy \(H=\log k\). This is the maximum entropy principle. For continuous distributions with fixed variance, the Gaussian maximizes differential entropy.

answer.text: \(X\) has a uniform distribution over its support

questiontext.text: For a continuous-time Markov chain (CTMC) with generator matrix \(Q\) (where \(Q_{ii}=-\lambda_i\) and \(Q_{ij}=q_{ij}\geq 0\) for \(i\neq j\)), the Kolmogorov forward equations are

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(\frac{d}{dt}P(t)=P(t)Q\)

The forward equations \(P'(t)=P(t)Q\) have solution \(P(t)=e^{Qt}\). The backward equations are \(P'(t)=QP(t)\). The stationary distribution \(\pi\) satisfies \(\pi Q=\mathbf{0}\), \(\sum_i\pi_i=1\). For birth-death CTMCs, detailed balance \(\pi_i q_{ij}=\pi_j q_{ji}\) applies.

questiontext.text: The EM algorithm for maximum likelihood with incomplete data alternates between the E-step (computing \(Q(\theta|\theta^{(t)})=E[\log L(\theta;\mathbf{X}_{complete})|\mathbf{X}_{obs},\theta^{(t)}]\)) and the M-step (maximizing \(Q\)). It is guaranteed that

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) the observed-data log-likelihood \(\ell(\theta^{(t+1)})\geq\ell(\theta^{(t)})\) at each step

Each EM iteration does not decrease the observed log-likelihood. This monotone ascent is proven via Jensen's inequality applied to the log. EM converges to a local maximum (or saddle point) but may converge slowly near the optimum.

answer.text: It always finds the global MLE
The observed-data log-likelihood is non-decreasing at each step
The complete-data log-likelihood decreases at each step
It converges in a fixed number of steps

questiontext.text: A compound Poisson random variable \(S=\sum_{i=1}^{N}X_i\), where \(N\sim\text{Poisson}(\lambda)\) and \(X_i\) i.i.d. with mean \(\mu_X\) and variance \(\sigma_X^2\), has variance

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(\lambda(\sigma_X^2+\mu_X^2)=\lambda E[X^2]\)

For a compound Poisson: \(E[S]=\lambda\mu_X\) and \(\text{Var}(S)=\lambda E[X^2]=\lambda(\sigma_X^2+\mu_X^2)\). This follows from the law of total variance: \(\text{Var}(S)=E[N]\text{Var}(X)+\text{Var}(N)(E[X])^2=\lambda\sigma_X^2+\lambda\mu_X^2\).

questiontext.text: Doob's \(L^p\) maximal inequality for a non-negative sub-martingale \(\{X_n\}\) (\(p>1\)) states

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(E[\max_{k\leq n}X_k^p]\leq\left(\frac{p}{p-1}\right)^p E[X_n^p]\)

Doob's maximal inequality bounds the expected maximum of a sub-martingale: \(\|M_n^*\|_p\leq\frac{p}{p-1}\|X_n\|_p\) where \(M_n^*=\max_{k\leq n}X_k\). For \(p=2\): \(E[(M_n^*)^2]\leq 4E[X_n^2]\). Fundamental in martingale convergence theory.

questiontext.text: The Wald test statistic for \(H_0:\theta=\theta_0\) (based on MLE \(\hat\theta\) with estimated Fisher information \(\hat I\)) is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(W=(\hat\theta-\theta_0)^2\hat I\sim\chi^2(1)\) asymptotically under \(H_0\)

The Wald statistic \(W=(\hat\theta-\theta_0)^T[\text{Var}(\hat\theta)]^{-1}(\hat\theta-\theta_0)\) is asymptotically \(\chi^2(r)\) under \(H_0\) (where \(r\) is the dimension of \(\theta\)). The Wald, score (Rao), and LR tests are asymptotically equivalent first-order.

questiontext.text: The Dominated Convergence Theorem (DCT) states: if \(X_n\to X\) a.s. and \(|X_n|\leq Y\) a.s. for all \(n\) with \(E[Y]<\infty\), then

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(E[X_n]\to E[X]\)

DCT (Lebesgue): the domination condition \(|X_n|\leq Y\in L^1\) allows interchanging limit and expectation. Also: \(E[|X_n-X|]\to 0\) (convergence in \(L^1\)). The DCT is fundamental in analysis and probability, allowing interchange of limit and integral.

answer.text: \(X_n\to X\) in probability but not a.s.

questiontext.text: In two-phase (double) sampling, the first phase selects a large sample to estimate auxiliary information, and the second phase selects a sub-sample. This design is used when

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) auxiliary data are expensive to collect but useful for stratification or estimation

Two-phase sampling: Phase 1 collects cheap auxiliary data \(x\) on a large sample \(n'\); Phase 2 collects expensive \(y\) on a sub-sample \(n\). The Phase-1 information improves Phase-2 estimation (e.g., via stratification based on \(x\), or ratio/regression estimation with Phase-1 means).

answer.text: Auxiliary data are expensive to collect but useful for improving efficiency
All units have equal inclusion probabilities

questiontext.text: A pivotal quantity is a function \(g(X,\theta)\) whose distribution is

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) free of all unknown parameters

A pivot has a known distribution not depending on \(\theta\) (or any other nuisance parameter). Example: \((\bar X-\mu)/(S/\sqrt{n})\sim t(n-1)\) for normal data — the distribution is \(t\) regardless of \(\mu,\sigma^2\). Pivots are used to construct exact confidence intervals.

questiontext.text: If \(N\) is a random variable independent of i.i.d. \(X_1,X_2,\ldots\), then \(\text{Var}(\sum_{i=1}^N X_i)\) equals

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(E[N]\text{Var}(X)+\text{Var}(N)(E[X])^2\)

By the law of total variance: \(\text{Var}(S_N)=E[\text{Var}(S_N|N)]+\text{Var}(E[S_N|N])=E[N\text{Var}(X)]+\text{Var}(N\mu_X)=E[N]\sigma_X^2+\mu_X^2\text{Var}(N)\). For \(N\sim\text{Poisson}(\lambda)\): \(\text{Var}(S_N)=\lambda E[X^2]\).

questiontext.text: The law of total variance states that for random variables \(X\) and \(Y\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (b)

The law of total variance (Eve's law) decomposes variance as \(\text{Var}(X)=E[\text{Var}(X|Y)]+\text{Var}(E[X|Y])\), separating within-group and between-group variation.

questiontext.text: Let \((X_1,X_2)\sim N_2(\mu,\Sigma)\) with \(\Sigma=\begin{pmatrix}\sigma_1^2 & \rho\sigma_1\sigma_2\\ \rho\sigma_1\sigma_2 & \sigma_2^2\end{pmatrix}\). The conditional distribution of \(X_1|X_2=x_2\) is normal with mean:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (c)

The conditional mean is \(\mu_1+\rho\frac{\sigma_1}{\sigma_2}(x_2-\mu_2)\) and conditional variance is \(\sigma_1^2(1-\rho^2)\). The correlation \(\rho\) governs the regression of \(X_1\) on \(X_2\).

questiontext.text: According to the Neyman-Fisher factorization theorem, a statistic \(T(X)\) is sufficient for parameter \(\theta\) if and only if the joint density can be written as:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (a)

The factorization criterion states \(f(x;\theta)=g(T(x);\theta)\cdot h(x)\), where \(g\) depends on \(x\) only through \(T(x)\) and \(h\) does not depend on \(\theta\). This characterizes sufficiency.

questiontext.text: The Lehmann-Scheffé theorem guarantees that if \(T\) is a complete sufficient statistic and \(\delta(T)\) is an unbiased estimator of \(\theta\), then \(\delta(T)\) is the:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (b)

Lehmann-Scheffé: an unbiased estimator based on a complete sufficient statistic is the Uniformly Minimum Variance Unbiased Estimator (UMVUE). Completeness rules out other unbiased functions of \(T\) with lower variance.

answer.text: Bayes estimator under squared error loss

questiontext.text: In simple linear regression \(Y=\beta_0+\beta_1 X+\varepsilon\), the coefficient of determination \(R^2\) satisfies:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (d)

\(R^2=\text{SSR}/\text{SST}=1-\text{SSE}/\text{SST}\in[0,1]\). It equals the square of the sample correlation \(r^2\) in simple linear regression and measures the proportion of total variance explained by the model.

answer.text: \(R^2=1-\text{SSE}/\text{SST}\in[0,1]\) and equals the square of the sample correlation \(r^2\)

name.text: Hypothesis Testing - Type I and Type II Errors

questiontext.text: In hypothesis testing, if the significance level is \(\alpha\) and the power of the test is \(1-\beta\), which statement is correct?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (c)

The size \(\alpha=P(\text{reject }H_0|H_0\text{ true})\) is the Type I error. \(\beta=P(\text{fail to reject }H_0|H_1\text{ true})\) is the Type II error. Power \(=1-\beta\). For fixed \(n\), decreasing \(\alpha\) increases \(\beta\); they cannot both be decreased simultaneously without increasing sample size.

answer.text: Decreasing \(\alpha\) decreases \(\beta\) for fixed sample size
\(\alpha\) and \(\beta\) can both be made arbitrarily small without changing \(n\)

questiontext.text: The Mann-Whitney U test is a nonparametric test for comparing two independent samples. Under \(H_0\) that the two populations have the same distribution with samples of sizes \(m\) and \(n\), the expected value of the U statistic is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (b)

The Mann-Whitney U statistic has \(E[U]=mn/2\) and \(\text{Var}(U)=mn(m+n+1)/12\) under \(H_0\). It is equivalent to the Wilcoxon rank-sum test and tests whether \(P(X>Y)=1/2\).

questiontext.text: Convergence in \(r\)-th mean (\(L^r\) convergence), \(E[|X_n-X|^r]\to 0\), and almost sure convergence are related as follows:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (a)

\(L^r\) convergence implies convergence in probability. Almost sure convergence also implies convergence in probability, but \(L^r\) convergence and a.s. convergence are independent (neither implies the other in general). Convergence in probability is the weakest of the three modes.

answer.text: Both \(L^r\) convergence and a.s. convergence imply convergence in probability, but neither implies the other
Almost sure convergence implies \(L^r\) convergence for all \(r\geq 1\)

questiontext.text: A Brownian bridge \(\{B_t^0\}_{0\leq t\leq 1}\) is defined as \(B_t^0=W_t-tW_1\) where \(W_t\) is standard Brownian motion. The covariance \(\text{Cov}(B_s^0,B_t^0)\) for \(s\leq t\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (c)

\(\text{Cov}(B_s^0,B_t^0)=\text{Cov}(W_s-sW_1,W_t-tW_1)=s-st=s(1-t)\) for \(s\leq t\). The Brownian bridge is pinned at 0 at both \(t=0\) and \(t=1\), and appears in the Kolmogorov-Smirnov test statistic's limiting distribution.

questiontext.text: In a renewal reward process where inter-renewal times have mean \(\mu\) and each renewal cycle earns reward with mean \(r\), the long-run average reward per unit time is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (a)

The renewal reward theorem states: long-run average reward \(=E[\text{reward per cycle}]/E[\text{cycle length}]=r/\mu\). This generalizes the elementary renewal theorem \(m(t)/t\to1/\mu\) to processes with rewards.

questiontext.text: To simulate a random variable \(X\) with CDF \(F\), the inverse transform method generates \(U\sim\text{Uniform}(0,1)\) and sets \(X=F^{-1}(U)\). This works because:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (b)

If \(U\sim U(0,1)\), then \(P(F^{-1}(U)\leq x)=P(U\leq F(x))=F(x)\), so \(F^{-1}(U)\) has CDF \(F\). This is the probability integral transform in reverse and works for any continuous CDF.

answer.text: It only works when \(F\) is strictly increasing on all of \(\mathbb{R}\)

questiontext.text: The Gumbel distribution arises as the limiting distribution of the maximum of \(n\) i.i.d. random variables from distributions with exponential-type tails. Its CDF is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (d)

The Gumbel (Type I extreme value) CDF is \(F(x)=\exp(-e^{-x})\). The three types of extreme value distributions (Gumbel, Fréchet, Weibull) are unified in the Generalized Extreme Value (GEV) distribution. The Gumbel arises for normal, exponential, and gamma parent distributions.

questiontext.text: Suppose \(X_1,\ldots,X_n|\theta\sim\text{Poisson}(\theta)\) and the prior is \(\theta\sim\text{Gamma}(\alpha,\beta)\) (mean \(\alpha\beta\)). The posterior mean of \(\theta\) given \(\bar{X}\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (c)

With Gamma prior \(\text{Gamma}(\alpha,\beta)\) and Poisson likelihood, the posterior is \(\text{Gamma}(\alpha+n\bar{X},\beta/(n\beta+1))\). The posterior mean is \((\alpha+n\bar{X})/(n+1/\beta)\). This is a weighted average of prior mean \(\alpha\beta\) and MLE \(\bar{X}\), demonstrating Bayesian shrinkage.

answer.text: A weighted average of \(\alpha\beta\) and \(\bar{X}\), shrinking MLE toward the prior mean

questiontext.text: The generalized likelihood ratio test statistic is \(\Lambda=\sup_{\theta\in\Theta_0}L(\theta)/\sup_{\theta\in\Theta}L(\theta)\). Under \(H_0\), by Wilks' theorem, \(-2\log\Lambda\) converges in distribution to:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (a)

Wilks' theorem: under \(H_0\) and regularity conditions, \(-2\log\Lambda\xrightarrow{d}\chi^2(r)\) where \(r=\dim\Theta-\dim\Theta_0\) is the number of restrictions. This allows construction of asymptotically valid likelihood ratio tests.

questiontext.text: Let \(X_1,X_2,\ldots\) be i.i.d. random variables and \(\mathcal{T}=\bigcap_{n=1}^\infty\sigma(X_{n+1},X_{n+2},\ldots)\) be the tail \(\sigma\)-algebra. By Kolmogorov's 0-1 law, for any event \(A\in\mathcal{T}\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (b)

Kolmogorov's 0-1 law: for i.i.d. sequences, tail events have probability 0 or 1. Tail events (like \(\{\limsup X_n/n>c\}\), \(\{\sum X_n\text{ converges}\}\)) are independent of any finite collection of \(X_i\) and hence independent of themselves, forcing \(P(A)\in\{0,1\}\).

questiontext.text: Let \(\{M_n\}\) be a martingale with respect to \(\{\mathcal{F}_n\}\) and \(\tau\) a stopping time. Doob's optional stopping theorem guarantees \(E[M_\tau]=E[M_0]\) provided:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (c)

Doob's optional stopping requires: (i) \(E[\tau]<\infty\), (ii) increments are uniformly bounded, or (iii) \(E[\sup_n|M_{n\wedge\tau}|]<\infty\). Under these conditions, optional stopping preserves the martingale property. Without conditions, the theorem can fail (e.g., doubling strategy in gambling).

answer.text: \(\tau\) is always finite (no additional conditions required)

questiontext.text: If \(X\) has a Cauchy distribution with location 0 and scale 1, its characteristic function \(\phi_X(t)=E[e^{itX}]\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (a)

The standard Cauchy characteristic function is \(\phi_X(t)=e^{-|t|}\). This is not differentiable at \(t=0\), consistent with the fact that Cauchy has no finite moments. The Lévy-Khintchine representation shows it is a stable distribution with index \(\alpha=1\).
